front: Philosopher: formulated five proofs of God's existence (quinque viae) including the unmoved mover, first cause, contingency, degree, and teleology - notably excluding the ontological argument
Philosopher: the fourth of his five ways uses the analogy of a 'worm lower down' and a 'dog higher' to argue that varying degrees of perfection necessitate a maximally perfect being
Philosopher: formulated the doctrine of double effect, arguing that 'nothing hinders one act from having two effects' and harmful side effects may be permissible if unintended
Philosopher: Philippa Foot's trolley problem paper titled 'The Problem of Abortion and the Doctrine of the Double Effect' popularized this thinker's principle
Philosopher: distinguished four kinds of law (eternal, natural, human, divine) and defined law as 'an ordinance of reason for the common good'
work: Natural Law and the Treatise on Law

front: Philosopher: John Finnis argued this thinker derived all norms from the self-evident first precept: 'good is to be done and pursued, and evil is to be avoided'
work: Natural Law and the Treatise on Law

front: Philosopher: listed just purpose, proper authority, and right intention as criteria for a just war, building on Augustine's original formulation
work: Natural Law and the Treatise on Law

front: Work: its question-and-answer format responds to objections with 'sed contra'; refers to Aristotle as 'The Philosopher' and Averroes as 'The Commentator'
Philosopher: Leo XIII's encyclical Aeterni Patris revived study of this thinker; John Haldane later developed 'analytical' version of his school blending it with analytic philosophy
Philosopher: Roberto Busa's partnership with IBM to index this thinker's writings produced a 56-volume work often credited as the first project of digital humanities
Work: written for missionaries and deliberately refrains from citing the Old or New Testament; split into human truths and divine truths
Philosopher: proposed the Analogia Entis ('Analogy of Being'), arguing that God's properties are predicated analogically, not univocally - Karl Barth called this idea 'the antichrist'
Philosopher: argued that essence and existence are distinct in all beings except God, building on Avicenna; used the example of healthy urine vs. healthy bodies
Philosopher: originated the Peripatetic Axiom: 'nothing is in the intellect that was not first in the senses'
Philosopher: argued the soul is 'the form of the body' in a commentary on Aristotle's De Anima and rejected plurality of substantial forms on grounds it fails to account for human unity
Philosopher: described beauty in terms of integritas, consonantia, and claritas; Umberto Eco argued his theory of aesthetic perception was strongly cognitive
Philosopher: Pope Urban IV commissioned five Corpus Christi hymns from this thinker, including 'Pange lingua' (basis for Josquin's last mass) and 'Adoro te devote'
Philosopher: disputed with Siger de Brabant at the University of Paris and argued against the 'unity of the intellect' proposed by Averroes
